title: Top Five Murder Mysteries. Plus: Rian Johnson on ‘Knives Out’
format: Sean and Amanda run dueling murder-mystery top fives, one a strict Agatha Christie constructionist and one a looser definition, then Rian Johnson explains how he built Knives Out.

films.title: Murder on the Orient Express (1974)
films.note: Sidney Lumet's all-star Poirot. Amanda still rates it a classic; the recent remake didn't do much for her.

films.title: Laura
films.note: Otto Preminger noir Amanda calls a major influence on the 70s films she loves.

films.title: Anatomy of a Murder
films.note: Preminger again, with Jimmy Stewart. A top-100 pick for Amanda, on the strength of the Duke Ellington score and performances from Lee Remick and Ben Gazzara.

films.title: Memories of Murder
films.note: Bong Joon-ho's breakthrough, newly relevant since Neon picked up the rights off Parasite's success. A docudrama about Korea's first serial-killer case that Amanda finds clever but never quite satisfying.

films.title: Death on the Nile
films.note: Ustinov's Poirot in Egypt, with a cast stacked to absurdity: Bette Davis, Mia Farrow, Maggie Smith, Angela Lansbury. A childhood favorite; the poster still hangs in her office.

films.title: Gosford Park
films.note: Altman and Julian Fellowes take the country-house whodunit and load real class politics underneath it.

films.title: Rear Window
films.note: Not technically a whodunit, but Hitchcock's photograph-comparison sleuthing is straight out of the Christie playbook.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: The film they're here to talk about. Amanda breaks her own rule and puts a week-old release on the list.

films.title: Evil Under the Sun
films.note: Ustinov's Poirot again, all cocktail hour and Cole Porter, never too solemn about its own murder.

films.title: Clue
films.note: Amanda's honorable mention, tacked on after the countdown: a parody of the genre rather than a real entry in it, carried by Tim Curry.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Murder Mysteries
segments.note: best to worst. His actual No. 4, the true-crime docuseries The Staircase, is left off this list since it's television, not a film
segments.subject: Murder Mysteries

films.title: Chinatown
films.note: Sean's favorite, freshly relevant with the Fincher/Robert Towne prequel series in development.

films.title: The Big Sleep
films.note: Bogart and Bacall, a plot so tangled even William Faulkner's rewrite didn't fully sort it out, which is the appeal for Sean.

films.title: Zodiac
films.note: The whodunit that never dunit; the obsession is the actual subject.

films.title: The Last of Sheila
films.note: Written by Stephen Sondheim and Anthony Perkins, with an all-star 70s cast (James Coburn, James Mason, Dyan Cannon, Raquel Welch) that Sean wishes Hollywood still made.

films.title: Murder Mystery
films.note: Adam Sandler and Jennifer Aniston's Netflix riff on the genre, shot partly in Monaco. Broad, not especially clever, but neither host disliked it.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: Johnson had the shape of the story about a decade before making it. He cast up every role the way the Ustinov-era Christie adaptations did, and gave Benoit Blanc a Southern accent so he'd be a fish out of water in New England.

films.title: Brick
films.note: Johnson's own read: a film-noir detective story, not a whodunit (“the only thing they have in common is the word detective.”)

films.title: The Brothers Bloom
films.note: A con-artist story Johnson frames as its own kind of mystery box: who's really running the scheme.

films.title: Looper
films.note: Fits the same pattern, per Johnson: who's responsible for what's happened to these characters.

films.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
films.note: Johnson calls the Rey/Kylo mystery-box angle “a little debatable” but allows the comparison.

films.title: Murder on the Orient Express (2017)
films.note: Branagh's version, one of the recent whodunits Johnson says he “really dug.”

films.title: Crooked House
films.note: A Glenn Close vehicle Johnson name-checks as one of the better recent Christie adaptations.

films.title: I Knew Her Well
films.note: An Italian black-and-white comedy Johnson caught on a friend's recommendation while on his festival run.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: One of the last great things Johnson had seen on tour with Knives Out.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Caught a screening; called it fantastic.

films.title: Game Night
films.note: Watched it on a plane and loved it: “the energy of that movie is kind of similar” to Knives Out, and both films happen to share the same fluffy white dog, Olivia.

Books: The Mystery of the Blue Train
